perturbation.original_text: 2.6 "Sponsorship Benefits" means the benefits Newegg will provide Allied, including the license granted in Section 6.1 and the fee stated in Schedule 3.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.6 "Sponsorship Benefits" means benefits Allied will provide Newegg, including advertising and promotion of Newegg Marks by Allied and the Sponsorship Rights set out in Schedule 4.
perturbation.explanation: This change contradicts the fundamental understanding of who provides the Sponsorship Benefits to whom. Originally, it was clear that Newegg provides the benefits, but the modified definition now indicates it's Allied providing them, which directly conflicts with section 4.2: 'Newegg shall provide to Allied the Sponsorship Benefits set out in Schedule 3, including paying the Sponsorship Fee as provided in that Schedule.' This in-text contradiction in the definition causes uncertainty about Newegg's financial commitment and creates confusion on which party is providing benefits. Section 4.1 references section 5.1 as license given to Newegg, which makes Allied as the providing party and Newegg as receiving party. This perturbation in-text is contradicting.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.2 Newegg shall provide to Allied the Sponsorship Benefits set out in Schedule 3, including paying the Sponsorship Fee as provided in that Schedule. Any value-added, goods and services, or similar tax or duty imposed by any government or tax authority on any Sponsorship Benefit shall be borne solely by Allied.
perturbation.justification: The change in the definition of "Sponsorship Benefits" from Newegg providing them to Allied providing them directly contradicts section 4.2, which states "Newegg shall provide to Allied the Sponsorship Benefits". This creates a fundamental conflict regarding who is responsible for providing the benefits and paying the Sponsorship Fee, leading to uncertainty and potential unenforceability. The change breaks the compatibility between the definition and the obligation outlined in section 4.2.

perturbation.original_text: 7.1 In all of Allied's actions and publications (in all media and formats) in connection with the marketing and conducting of the Events, where possible and appropriate, Allied shall where reasonably practicable communicate that Newegg is the exclusive sponsor of the Arena for the technology e-commerce and online retailer categories.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1 In all of Allied's actions and publications (in all media and formats) in connection with the marketing and conducting of the Events, Allied may, at its sole discretion, communicate that Newegg is a sponsor of the Arena. Allied is under no obligation to communicate exclusivity, and may promote other technology e-commerce and online retailer sponsors.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the obligation for Allied to communicate Newegg's exclusivity and introduces the possibility of promoting competitors. This directly contradicts the intention of the original text which focused on exclusivity, creating an in-text contradiction with the originally stated benefit of being the 'exclusive sponsor'. This is contradicting and creating uncertaingy with section 7.2: 'Allied shall not endorse, or permit the marketing of any other company whose principal business is as an e-commerce provider at or in connection with the Arena.' which mentions the e-commerce provider.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.2 Allied shall not endorse, or permit the marketing of any other company whose principal business is as an e-commerce provider at or in connection with the Arena. For purposes of clarity, this is not meant to prohibit incidental third-party endorsements not controlled by Allied such as individual player and team sponsorships of participants in events at the Arena.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 7.1 removes the obligation for Allied to communicate Newegg's exclusivity as the sponsor, explicitly stating that Allied may promote other technology e-commerce and online retailer sponsors. This directly contradicts section 7.2, which states 'Allied shall not endorse, or permit the marketing of any other company whose principal business is as an e-commerce provider at or in connection with the Arena.' The change creates a clear conflict because Allied is now explicitly permitted to promote competitors, while the contradicted section prohibits endorsing other e-commerce providers.

perturbation.original_text: 14.2 Each Party may also terminate the Agreement for convenience after Contract Year 2 (as defined in Section 14.4) by providing written notice to the other Party at least sixty (60) calendar days prior to the effective date of such termination
perturbation.changed_text: 14.2 Each Party may also terminate the Agreement for convenience after Contract Year 2 (as defined in Section 14.4) by providing written notice to the other Party at least sixty (60) calendar days prior to the effective date of such termination. Newegg, however, can terminate the agreement for convenience at anytime without any prior notice.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by granting Newegg the right to terminate the agreement at any time without prior notice, whereas the original text requires a 60-day notice. This undermines the original termination clause by creating an in-text contradiction, and Newegg is being favored to terminate without no conditions, as opposed to Allied, who have to comply with the pre-existing conditions. The added statement regarding Newegg creates in-text contradictions with section 14.3, which is also about terminating the agreement, by not following all the conditions that ALL parties should follow to terminate an agreement, but Newegg can't.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 14.3 Except as provided in (i) Section 5.2 for immediate termination subject to cure provisions in Section 14.1 , (ii) Section 14.1 for termination following notice, and (iii) Section 14.5 for immediate termination without notice, if either Party defaults in the performance, or breaches any provision, of this Agreement, then the non- defaulting Party may give written notice to the defaulting Party requiring the default or breach to be cured, and if the default or breach is not cured within fifteen (15) days of the receipt of the notice, this Agreement shall, without prejudice to any accrued right, automatically terminate at the end of the fifteen (15) day period.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a provision that Newegg can terminate the agreement at any time without prior notice, directly contradicting the original requirement in Section 14.2 for a 60-day notice for termination. Furthermore, it contradicts Section 14.3, which outlines conditions for termination related to performance defaults and breaches, that apply to ALL parties. The added clause for Newegg introduces a specific exception for one party, creating an inconsistency that undermines the agreement's standard termination procedures and favors one party (Newegg) over the other (Allied), leading to a clear and direct conflict within the document.
